title: Tripleurospermum szowitzii
intro: Tripleurospermum szowitzii, also known as Matricaria discoidea, is a flowering plant belonging to the daisy family (Asteraceae). This plant, commonly referred to as wild chamomile or false chamomile, has been used in traditional medicine for its numerous health benefits, including soothing digestive issues, reducing anxiety and insomnia, and exhibiting anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Tripleurospermum szowitzii include apigenin, luteolin, and bisabolol, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Tripleurospermum szowitzii is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being incorporated into topical creams and salves for skin and wound care.
taxonomy: Tripleurospermum szowitzii is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It is a type of daisy, more specifically belonging to the genus Tripleurospermum. This genus is part of the larger tribe Anthemideae, which contains various daisy and sunflower-like species. Tripleurospermum szowitzii is native to parts of Europe, where it grows as a low-lying or spreading perennial herb. The classification of the plant reflects its evolutionary relationships within the broader plant kingdom.

side_effects: Tripleurospermum szowitzii is also known as bitterweed or wild mustard. However, there is not enough information available about the specific side effects of this plant.
